did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes DeepMetaForge, a deep learning framework using a Vision Transformer (BEiT) backbone and a Deep Metadata Fusion Module (DMFM) with a CNN for automatic skin lesion classification. It was evaluated on four public datasets of dermoscopic and smartphone skin lesion images to distinguish malignant from benign skin diseases.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper explicitly states 'This research paper is supported by...' and proposes a novel deep learning framework called DeepMetaForge. It details original experimental methods, including the use of BEiT and a Deep Metadata Fusion Module, and reports empirical results on four public datasets, demonstrating new technical contributions rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: DeepMetaForge: A Deep Vision-Transformer Metadata-Fusion Network for Automatic Skin Lesion Classification
brief_summary: This study proposes DeepMetaForge, a deep learning framework for automatic skin lesion classification that integrates dermoscopic and smartphone images with patient metadata. The architecture utilizes a BEiT Vision Transformer backbone for image encoding and a novel Deep Metadata Fusion Module (DMFM) to simultaneously compress and blend visual features with metadata encoded by a CNN. The model was evaluated on four public datasets (ISIC 2020, PAD-UFES-20, SKINL2, and PH2) using a binary benign-versus-malignant classification task with 5-fold cross-validation. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed framework achieved a macro-average F1 score of 87.1% on average, outperforming state-of-the-art image classification backbones and metadata-fusing approaches. The study also conducted a scalability analysis, confirming the framework's viability for implementation on low-resource devices and as telemedicine services.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign or malignant categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'automatic skin lesion classification' and defines the prediction as a 'binary classification based on whether the skin lesion is benign or malignant.' The classification layer reduces features to a binary output. The primary objective is to 'enable the detection of malignant skin lesions by binary classification rather than focusing on multiclass classification.' Evaluation metrics include precision, recall, F1 score, accuracy, MCC, sensitivity, specificity, and NPV for the binary classes (positive=malignant, negative=benign).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic skin lesion images containing metadata such as gender, age approximation, anatomical site, and diagnosis.

use.training: Used for training the DeepMetaForge model with 70% of data split for training (further split into 8 parts for hyperparameter tuning).
use.validation: Used for validation with 10% of data split for hyperparameter tuning.
use.testing: Used for testing with 20% of data split.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of smartphone skin lesion images containing metadata such as smoking/drinking habits, age, pesticide exposure, gender, skin cancer history, Fitzpatrick score, region, lesion diameter, and symptoms.

use.training: Used for training the DeepMetaForge model with 70% of data split for training.
use.validation: Used for validation with 10% of data split.
use.testing: Used for testing with 20% of data split.

datasets_used.name: SKINL2
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic skin lesion images containing metadata such as gender, age, fototype, and melanocytic attributes.

use.training: Used for training the DeepMetaForge model with 70% of data split for training.
use.validation: Used for validation with 10% of data split.
use.testing: Used for testing with 20% of data split.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic skin lesion images containing metadata such as histological diagnosis, asymmetry, pigment network, dots/globules, streaks, regression areas, blue-whitish veil, and color characteristics.

use.training: Used for training the DeepMetaForge model with 70% of data split for training.
use.validation: Used for validation with 10% of data split.
use.testing: Used for testing with 20% of data split.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the DeepMetaForge model on four public datasets (ISIC 2020, PAD-UFES-20, SKINL2, and PH2).
To analyze the impact of individual components (compression branch, skip feature map) and determine the optimal compression ratio for the Deep Metadata Fusion Module (DMFM).
To represent the overall classification efficacy by averaging the F1 scores of the benign and malignant classes.

statistical_methods_used.method: Comparison of performance metrics (Precision, Recall, Accuracy, MCC, Sensitivity, Specificity, NPV)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To provide a comprehensive analysis of the model's effectiveness and compare the proposed framework against state-of-the-art image classification backbones and metadata-fusing approaches.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using relative performance differences (delta F1) and absolute metric values (precision, recall, F1, accuracy, MCC, etc.) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2020.metric: Precision, Recall, F1 Score, Accuracy, MCC, Sensitivity, Specificity, NPV, Macro-average F1
ISIC 2020.notes: The best compression ratio for the DMFM was found to be 8, yielding a macro-average F1 of 92.0%. Incorporating actual metadata improved performance by 87.32% over image-only input. BEiT-base-384 achieved the highest macro-average F1 of 92.9%.

PAD-UFES-20.metric: Precision, Recall, F1 Score, Accuracy, MCC, Sensitivity, Specificity, NPV, Macro-average F1
PAD-UFES-20.notes: Incorporating metadata improved performance by only 1.51% over image-only input, indicating a less prominent effect of metadata on this dataset compared to others.

SKINL2.metric: Precision, Recall, F1 Score, Accuracy, MCC, Sensitivity, Specificity, NPV, Macro-average F1
SKINL2.notes: Incorporating metadata improved performance by 4.72% over image-only input.

PH2.metric: Precision, Recall, F1 Score, Accuracy, MCC, Sensitivity, Specificity, NPV, Macro-average F1
PH2.notes: Incorporating metadata improved performance by 10.10% over image-only input.

Average (Across All Datasets).notes: The proposed framework yielded an average macro-average F1 score of 87.1% across all datasets.

architecture_used.pretraining: BEiT (Bidirectional Encoder representation from Image Transformers) pre-trained as a masked image modeling (MIM) task
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels (RGB images resized to height and width of 224)

architecture_details.image_encoder: BEiT backbone that tokenizes original image into small visual tokens and randomly masks some image patches
architecture_details.metadata_encoder: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) consisting of two sets of convolutional layers, each including a one-dimensional convolutional filter, a normalizer (batch normalization), and an activation function (ReLU). First CNN converts input metadata into a feature map with 256 channels; second CNN converts output to match image encoder shape.
architecture_details.fusion_module: Deep Metadata Fusion Module (DMFM) which merges feature maps from image and metadata encoders. Uses a compression ratio (γ) of 8. Includes a compression branch and a skip feature map.
architecture_details.activation_functions: Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) and dropout with 0.4 probability
architecture_details.learning_rate_schedule: Initial learning rate 0.001, decay of 0.90 at every five epochs
architecture_details.augmentation: Normalization, random flipping, and random rotation

training_details.hardware: Single Linux machine with NVIDIA Geforce RTX 3090 GPU, Intel i7-12700K CPU, 32 GB memory
training_details.initialization: Network encoder pre-trained on original publication; other layers randomly initialized

classification_or_output_head.details: Reduces features to binary output (benign vs. malignant)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing RGB images to 224x224 pixels (with some tests using 384x384 depending on the pre-trained model architecture)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to input images as part of the data augmentation pipeline to handle diverse inputs.
Applied to input images as part of the data augmentation pipeline to allow the model to handle a range of diverse inputs.
Applied to input images as part of the data augmentation pipeline to allow the model to handle a range of diverse inputs.

conclusions: The proposed DeepMetaForge framework, which integrates a BEiT Vision Transformer backbone with a Deep Metadata Fusion Module (DMFM) and patient metadata, achieved an average macro-average F1 score of 87.1% across four public datasets (ISIC 2020, PAD-UFES-20, SKINL2, and PH2).

Incorporating actual patient metadata significantly improved classification performance compared to image-only inputs, with improvements ranging from 1.51% on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset to 87.32% on the ISIC 2020 dataset, indicating that the impact of metadata varies by dataset.

The Deep Metadata Fusion Module (DMFM) with a compression ratio of 8 was identified as the most efficient configuration, yielding the highest macro-average F1 score of 92.0% on the ISIC 2020 dataset and improving the efficiency of feature concatenation by 31.2%.

The BEiT backbone outperformed other tested image classification backbones (ResNext, EfficientNet) and state-of-the-art metadata-fusing approaches, with the best configuration (BEiT-base-384 with actual metadata) achieving a macro-average F1 score of 92.9% on the ISIC 2020 dataset.

Scalability analysis demonstrated that the proposed framework is viable for implementation on low-resource devices and as telemedicine API services, suggesting its potential for nationwide skin cancer detection systems.

The study highlights the importance of evaluating metadata fusion methods across diverse datasets to ensure generalizability and robustness, as previous research often validated such methods on single datasets.
important_remarks: The paper is an author's version accepted for publication in IEEE Access and has not been fully edited; content may change prior to final publication.
The study is limited to a binary classification task (benign vs. malignant) rather than multiclass classification, which limits the granularity of diagnostic insights.

Evaluation was conducted exclusively on four public datasets (ISIC 2020, PAD-UFES-20, SKINL2, PH2); there is no external validation on independent, private, or clinical datasets from different institutions.
The PAD-UFES-20 dataset consists of smartphone images, while others are dermoscopic; performance variability across these diverse image sources highlights potential sensitivity to input modality.

The impact of metadata fusion varies significantly by dataset (e.g., 87.32% improvement on ISIC 2020 vs. only 1.51% on PAD-UFES-20), suggesting that metadata utility is highly dependent on the specific dataset's characteristics and metadata quality.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance comparisons rely on absolute metric values and relative differences without significance testing.

The scalability analysis is empirical and theoretical, demonstrating viability for low-resource devices and API services, but does not include real-world deployment trials or latency measurements in clinical settings.

The study does not address potential data leakage risks from metadata (e.g., anatomical site or diagnosis codes) that might correlate with image features, which could artificially inflate performance.
The framework was tested on a single hardware configuration (NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090); generalizability to other hardware architectures is not empirically verified.
